name: ICT-Enabled Citizen Accountability and Grievance Redressal
description: This domain examines how information and communication technologies enable citizens to monitor government spending, access local services, and hold bureaucracies accountable, as seen in initiatives like 'Our Money, Our Accounts' and Karnataka's helpline and Aasthi systems. It includes studies of voice-based citizen journalism platforms like CGNet Swara and broader analyses of ICT use in Indian governance. The readings also critically explore the surveillance dimensions of these technologies, addressing how bureaucrats both use and subvert digital tools.

background_concepts.concept: Social audit / MGNREGA public hearings (jan sunwai)
background_concepts.why_needed: The MKSS 'Hamara Paisa, Hamara Hisab' and helpline/accountability readings assume familiarity with India's social audit movement and the MGNREGA social-audit mechanism as the institutional backdrop for citizen accountability, but do not explain how these processes work.

suggested_resources.title: A Guide to Conducting Social Audits (Accountability India)
Social Audits Overview (Government of Meghalaya State Rural Employment Society)

name: Datafication of Informal Urban Settlements
description: This domain explores how data collection and datafication processes affect informal urban settlements and slums, particularly in the global south, through frameworks of data justice and case studies like Quarry Road West in Durban. It engages with debates over smart city citizenship, participation, and the power dynamics of big data in public service organizations. The readings collectively interrogate how making marginalized communities 'visible' through data can advance or undermine justice and rights.

background_concepts.why_needed: Several of these readings apply or extend an 'applied data justice framework' and invoke visibility/(dis)engagement/anti-discrimination pillars without teaching the underlying concept of data justice they build on.

suggested_resources.title: What Is Data Justice? (Royal Roads University LibGuide)
Framing data justice: Taylor's three pillars (primer)

name: Satellite Imagery for Development Measurement and Targeting
description: This domain covers the use of satellite imagery and remote sensing to measure socio-economic indicators, classify land cover, detect urban slums, and track the evolution of cities. It includes methodological work on datasets like IndiaSat and night-time luminosity as proxies for economic activity, as well as applications for targeting villages for rural development. The readings also extend to using imagery to assess regional inequality and the effects of international sanctions, as in North Korea.

background_concepts.concept: Nighttime lights (DMSP-OLS / VIIRS) as an economic proxy
background_concepts.why_needed: These readings use night-time luminosity as a stand-in for economic activity and specific sensors (DMSP-OLS, VIIRS) without explaining how the data is collected or why brightness correlates with GDP.

suggested_resources.title: Illuminating Economic Growth Using Satellite Images (IMF Finance & Development)
Light Every Night – nighttime light data set and tools (World Bank Data Blog)

background_concepts.concept: Supervised land-cover / pixel-level classification of satellite imagery
background_concepts.why_needed: The IndiaSat, urban-slum-detection and city-evolution papers assume knowledge of supervised land-cover classification, spectral bands, and training/ground-truth workflows that they use but don't introduce.

suggested_resources.title: ARSET – Land Cover Classification with Satellite Imagery (NASA)
Introduction to LULC Mapping – Land Use Land Cover Guide

name: Political Connections, Patronage, and Rent Capture
description: This domain investigates how firms benefit from political ties, focusing on the mechanisms by which connected companies gain preferential access to finance, contracts, and resources. Readings examine quid pro quo arrangements between builders and politicians in election finance, lender favoritism toward connected firms, and rent capture in specific industries such as India's sugar mills.

background_concepts.why_needed: The quantitative rent-capture papers estimate the causal effect of political connections using panel and difference-in-differences style designs and rely on the parallel-trends logic without explaining it.

suggested_resources.title: Difference-in-Differences (World Bank DIME Wiki)
An Introduction to Difference-in-Difference Analysis (Tilburg Science Hub)

name: Electoral Cycles in Public Service Provision
description: This domain analyzes how electoral timing influences the delivery of public services, with a specific focus on electricity provision and losses in India. Using quasi-experimental evidence from special elections, the readings demonstrate how political incentives shape patterns of service allocation and utility performance around election cycles.

background_concepts.why_needed: Both papers exploit election timing as a natural experiment and rely on quasi-experimental estimators (RDD, DiD) whose identifying assumptions are assumed rather than taught.

suggested_resources.title: Regression Discontinuity Designs in Economics (Lee & Lemieux user guide)
Difference-in-Differences (World Bank DIME Wiki)

background_concepts.why_needed: Min's work measures 'electricity losses' as the political manipulation variable, which requires understanding technical vs. commercial T&D loss as a metric — used without definition.

suggested_resources.title: Light Every Night – measuring energy access with nighttime lights (World Bank)

name: Elite Power and Structural Inequality (Foundational Critique)
description: This domain provides foundational theoretical critiques of concentrated power and economic inequality, drawing on classic works like Mills' 'The Power Elite' and Domhoff's class-domination theory of power. It includes analyses of rising inequality in wealth and its societal dangers, as articulated by Piketty and Stiglitz, alongside examinations of persistent poverty amid growing affluence in the new India.

name: E-Participation and Digital Democracy Platforms
description: This domain examines the design and adoption of digital platforms intended to foster citizen engagement and participation in governance across cities like Windhoek, Cape Town, and Mexico. The readings analyze factors affecting citizens' uptake of e-participation tools and how such platforms can address mistrust while strengthening democratic practices.

name: Digital Identity, Documentation, and Exclusion
description: This domain explores how digital identification and documentation systems, such as India's Aadhaar and birth declaration processes in rural Bangladesh, affect access to rights and services. The readings highlight how these systems can both serve social goals like reducing child marriage and produce exclusions that marginalize vulnerable communities.

background_concepts.why_needed: The MarginalizedAadhaar documentary and related readings assume the reader knows how Aadhaar enrolment, biometric authentication, and its linkage to services work when documenting denial-of-service exclusions.

suggested_resources.title: Aadhaar: A Complete Guide to India's Unique Identification System
ID systems analysed: Aadhaar (Privacy International)

name: ICT for Refugee and Stateless Populations
description: This domain focuses on how information and communication technologies support Rohingya refugees in Bangladesh, addressing themes of solidarity, leadership, negotiation, and hope. The readings examine refugees' aspirations for democratic participation and self-determination in the digital age amid statelessness.

name: Media, Information, and Policy Discourse
description: This domain analyzes the role of traditional and digital media in shaping political discourse, polarization, and policy debates. It includes studies of the political blogosphere and Twitter polarization in the US context, alongside analyses of media bias in Indian policy discourse and the media's role in monitoring social welfare schemes.

name: Open Data and Aid Transparency
description: This domain examines how open data practices are transforming the international aid sector, as reflected in readings like 'How open data is changing international aid' and 'The next web.' It also covers methods for classifying and tracking aid contributions in relation to development goals, drawing on 'Classifying and Tracking International Aid Contribution Towards SDGs.'

name: Community Telecenters and Intermediaries
description: This domain explores the role of community-based telecenters and multimedia centers in expanding technology access, with particular attention to the pivotal yet complicated role of human intermediaries. Grounded in readings on 'The Complex Position of the Intermediary in Telecenters' and the 'Sustainability Failures of Rural Telecenters' from the SARI project, it interrogates the operational and sustainability challenges these initiatives face in rural settings.

name: Digital delivery of social protection entitlements
description: This domain investigates how digital systems, including India's Aadhaar identification program, mediate the delivery of social protection benefits and often produce exclusion, as seen in 'MarginalizedAadhaar' and 'Technologies that Disempower.' It examines design flaws, grievance redress, and citizen-assistance mechanisms, while also considering broader economic shocks like the effects of demonetization on domestic agricultural trade.

background_concepts.concept: Public Distribution System (PDS) and Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT)
background_concepts.why_needed: This domain's readings assume how India's entitlement pipelines (PDS rations, DBT subsidies/pensions) authenticate and pay beneficiaries — the institutional mechanism into which authentication is inserted — without explaining it.

suggested_resources.title: Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT): JAM Trinity Explained
What Is Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT)? (Bank of Baroda)

name: Technology-assisted grievance redress and citizen engagement in welfare programs
description: This domain focuses on technological and civil-society mechanisms for improving transparency, accountability, and citizen participation in social welfare schemes, exemplified by systems like 'Airavat' and technology-assisted grievance redressal models in rural India. It also considers the roles of citizen engagement, mass media monitoring, and systemic reform in strengthening the performance of welfare and education programs.

background_concepts.concept: Grievance redressal mechanism (institutional structure in Indian governance)
background_concepts.why_needed: These readings build feedback and complaint loops on top of India's formal grievance redress architecture (nodal officers, escalation, CPGRAMS-style portals); they assume readers know how a government grievance mechanism is supposed to function.

suggested_resources.title: CPGRAMS — Centralised Public Grievance Redress and Monitoring System (official portal)
Grievance Redress Mechanism in Government (DFE/Government of India guide, PDF)

background_concepts.concept: Social audit as a social-accountability mechanism
background_concepts.why_needed: Civil-society-mediated redress and transparency systems for welfare schemes draw on the social-audit tradition (Gram Sabha verification of records against ground reality under MGNREGA Section 17); the readings assume this participatory-accountability model.

suggested_resources.title: Social Audits in MGNREGS (Drishti IAS)
A Guide to Conducting Social Audits (Accountability India, PDF)

background_concepts.concept: Welfare scheme entitlements (PDS/MGNREGA/pensions) that grievances concern
background_concepts.why_needed: Building engagement into 'welfare schemes' presumes the reader knows the specific entitlement programs (rations under NFSA, employment under MGNREGA) whose exclusion errors generate the complaints being surfaced.

suggested_resources.title: National Food Security Act, 2013 (BYJU'S IAS notes)

name: Satellite imagery and geospatial ML for governance and monitoring
description: This domain applies satellite imagery and geospatial machine learning to governance, humanitarian, and monitoring challenges, including predicting socio-economic indicators, tracking urban growth in flood-risk areas, and detecting small vessels or brick kilns. Through tools like 'SatDash,' 'VulnerabilityMap,' and 'Space to Policy,' it demonstrates how planetary-scale geospatial data can support policy compliance, disaster assessment, and mapping of vulnerable populations.

background_concepts.concept: Supervised classification / semantic segmentation of satellite imagery
background_concepts.why_needed: The urban-growth monitoring, land-damage, and vessel-detection systems apply pixel-level classification and segmentation of remote-sensing imagery as a core method that is assumed rather than introduced.

suggested_resources.title: ARSET – Land Cover Classification with Satellite Imagery (NASA)
Tutorial: Remote Sensing Image Classification with QGIS (OCWGIS)
